title: What Aeromere Does
text: Aeromere is a short-term conflict-detection and resolution-advisory engine that sector controllers run alongside their primary radar and flight-data displays, predicting where two aircraft's trajectories will come closer than allowed separation before that loss of separation actually develops. It fuses radar track updates with ADS-B broadcast position reports, reconciling the two sources when they disagree rather than trusting either exclusively, since a single feed occasionally suffers dropouts or multipath errors near terrain. Aeromere projects each aircraft's near-term trajectory forward using its current heading, speed, and any active flight-plan intent, then checks every pairwise combination of projected trajectories within a sector for a predicted separation violation. When a probable conflict is found, the system proposes one or more resolution advisories, a heading change, altitude change, or speed adjustment, ranked by how little they disrupt each aircraft's filed route.

title: Trajectory Prediction
text: Aeromere's trajectory predictor extrapolates an aircraft's position over a rolling look-ahead window using a kinematic model rather than a full aerodynamic simulation, trading some accuracy on maneuvering aircraft for a prediction fast enough to recompute every sector continuously. Straight, level flight is projected with high confidence using current ground speed and track; a climbing, descending, or turning aircraft is instead matched against the filed flight plan's next waypoint and cleared altitude, blending the kinematic extrapolation with that intent data as confidence in the intent source increases. Uncertainty grows with look-ahead time, so Aeromere widens a protected volume around each projected position the further into the future it extrapolates, rather than treating a twelve-minute prediction with the same confidence as a two-minute one.

title: Separation Minima Enforcement
text: Aeromere encodes separation minima as configurable rules rather than fixed constants, since required separation varies by airspace class, aircraft category, and whether both aircraft are under radar or procedural control. In radar-controlled en-route airspace, the default lateral minimum is 5 nautical miles with a 1,000-foot vertical minimum below flight level 410, widening to 2,000 feet above that level in non-reduced-vertical-separation-minimum airspace. Approach control sectors typically tighten the lateral minimum to 3 nautical miles given denser, slower traffic and more precise radar coverage close to the airport. Aeromere's rule engine selects the applicable minima automatically based on each aircraft's current sector and altitude band, and a sector supervisor can override the active ruleset only through a logged, dual-authorization change rather than a single click.

title: Sensor Fusion Statistics
text: Across Aeromere's twelve production sector deployments, radar and ADS-B position reports for the same aircraft disagree by more than 0.1 nautical miles in roughly 3.4% of paired samples, most commonly during turns when ADS-B's higher update rate captures a position radar has not yet swept. The fusion module resolves 96.7% of these disagreements automatically using a confidence-weighted blend, escalating the remaining 3.3% to a controller-visible discrepancy flag. Mean end-to-end latency from raw sensor update to a refreshed trajectory projection is 410 milliseconds, measured across the 2025 production deployments. ADS-B coverage gaps, typically over sparsely instrumented terrain, affect an average of 2.1% of flight time per flight in the busiest deployed sector.

title: Conflict Detection Performance
text: Aeromere's twelve production sectors combined logged 41,800 predicted conflicts flagged for controller review during 2025, of which 38,600 were subsequently confirmed by controllers as genuine developing conflicts warranting an advisory. The remaining 3,200 were dismissed as false positives, a 7.7% false-positive rate, down from 14.2% measured on the 2022 production release before the trajectory predictor's intent-blending update shipped. Missed-detection rate, meaning a genuine loss of separation that Aeromere failed to flag with at least two minutes of advance warning, was measured at 0.03% across the same sample. Mean advisory lead time, from first flag to predicted loss of separation, was 4.6 minutes across confirmed conflicts.

title: Resolution Advisory Statistics
text: Of the 38,600 confirmed conflicts logged in 2025, controllers accepted Aeromere's top-ranked resolution advisory without modification in 71.4% of cases, modified the suggested heading or altitude before issuing it in 21.8% of cases, and disregarded the advisory entirely in favor of independent judgment in the remaining 6.8%. Heading-change advisories were the most frequently accepted resolution type at 58% of accepted advisories, followed by altitude-change advisories at 31% and speed-adjustment advisories at 11%. Median advisory computation time, from conflict confirmation to a ranked advisory list reaching the controller display, was 1.2 seconds. Aeromere logs every disregarded advisory along with the controller's alternative action for later review by sector training staff.

title: Sector Capacity Modeling
text: Aeromere feeds its conflict-density statistics into each deployment's sector capacity model, which estimates the maximum traffic count a sector can safely handle before controller workload, rather than airspace geometry alone, becomes the binding constraint. The current capacity model weighs predicted conflict rate more heavily than raw aircraft count, since two sectors with identical traffic counts can differ sharply in conflict rate depending on route geometry and crossing-traffic patterns. Sectors flagged as approaching their modeled capacity threshold trigger an advisory to traffic-flow management recommending a ground-delay program or reroute before the sector itself becomes saturated. The model is recalibrated quarterly against the prior quarter's actual controller workload survey data, rather than left static after initial deployment.

title: Radar and ADS-B Update Characteristics
text:
Radar and ADS-B differ meaningfully in update characteristics, which is precisely why Aeromere fuses rather than picks one source exclusively.

| Characteristic | Secondary radar | ADS-B |
|---|---|---|
| Typical update rate | 4-5 s | 0.5-1 s |
| Position accuracy | ~150 m | ~15 m |
| Terrain masking risk | Moderate | Low-moderate |
| Requires aircraft equipage | No | Yes |

ADS-B's faster, more accurate updates make it the preferred source when available and trusted, but Aeromere retains radar fusion specifically to cover aircraft with degraded or absent ADS-B equipage.

title: False-Positive Rate by Release
text:
False-positive conflict flags dropped sharply across three major Aeromere releases as the trajectory predictor's intent-blending logic matured.

| Release | Year | False-positive rate | Missed-detection rate |
|---|---|---|---|
| v2.0 | 2022 | 14.2% | 0.09% |
| v3.0 | 2023 | 10.1% | 0.06% |
| v4.0 | 2025 | 7.7% | 0.03% |

Missed-detection rate improved alongside the false-positive rate rather than trading against it, since better intent-blending sharpened trajectory accuracy in both directions rather than merely widening protected volumes to suppress false alarms.

title: Protected-Volume Sizing
text: Aeromere sizes the protected volume around a projected trajectory using r(t) = r0 + k * sqrt(t), where r0 is the minimum protected radius at the current time, t is look-ahead time in minutes, and k is an empirically fitted growth constant currently set to 0.35 nautical miles per square-root-minute for en-route airspace. The square-root growth reflects that position uncertainty accumulates roughly as a random walk under typical heading and speed variability, rather than growing linearly, which would overstate uncertainty at long look-ahead times and needlessly suppress advisories with adequate lead time. A conflict is flagged whenever two aircraft's protected volumes are predicted to intersect at any point within the look-ahead window, and the flag's urgency is ranked by the earliest predicted intersection time rather than by the volumes' degree of overlap.

title: International Trajectory Prediction Research
text: Aeromere's kinematic trajectory predictor draws on conflict-prediction research from several national air navigation research traditions beyond the English-language literature most avionics engineers cite first. Müller and Zellner's Unsicherheitsmodellierung bei der Flugbahnprädiktion im Streckenflug (2017) formalized the square-root growth model for trajectory uncertainty that Aeromere's protected-volume sizing still follows. A separate line of French-language research, summarized in Lefebvre's doctoral note, "L'intégration de l'intention de vol améliore sensiblement la précision de prédiction à moyen terme," directly motivated blending filed flight-plan intent into the kinematic model rather than relying on kinematics alone. Aeromere's engineering specification cites both works in its trajectory-prediction methodology appendix, alongside domestic research on radar-ADS-B fusion confidence weighting.

title: Sector Handoff Terminology
text: Terminology for sector-to-sector traffic handoff varies across air navigation service providers, which occasionally complicates cross-border conflict-detection coordination near shared boundaries. Portuguese-language procedural documentation commonly uses transferência de controlo (control transfer) for what English-language procedures call a handoff, a term Aeromere's cross-border interface maps directly. Turkish-language documentation instead distinguishes devir (a routine, uncontested handoff) from acil devir (an emergency or expedited handoff triggered by a developing conflict near the boundary), a distinction Aeromere's boundary-conflict logic mirrors internally even though its own interface exposes only a single English-language handoff-priority flag. Coordination working groups reviewing Aeromere deployments spanning two service providers have recommended exposing the Turkish distinction explicitly in a future release, rather than collapsing it into one flag.

title: Advisory Escalation Checklist
text:
When Aeromere flags a predicted loss of separation, the controller working position follows a fixed escalation sequence before any advisory reaches the flight deck. 1. Confirm the flagged conflict against the raw radar and ADS-B tracks rather than trusting the flag alone.
2. Review Aeromere's ranked resolution advisories and select, modify, or reject the top-ranked option.
3. Issue the selected instruction to one or both aircraft via the assigned frequency.
4. Confirm read-back and monitor the updated trajectory projection for confirmed separation.
5. Log the outcome, including any modification to the suggested advisory, for later review. A controller who rejects every proposed advisory must record a brief rationale, which sector training staff review during the following shift debrief.

title: New-Sector Commissioning Checklist
text:
Bringing a new sector onto Aeromere requires a fixed commissioning sequence before the sector may run the system live rather than in shadow mode. 1. Load the sector's airspace geometry, adjacent-sector boundaries, and applicable separation-minima ruleset.
2. Run at least four weeks of shadow-mode operation, logging Aeromere's advisories without displaying them to controllers.
3. Compare shadow-mode advisories against the sector's actual historical conflict resolutions for the same period.
4. Recalibrate the sector capacity model using the shadow-mode conflict-density data collected.
5. Obtain sign-off from both the sector's operations manager and the regional safety board before enabling live advisories. Sectors that fail the shadow-mode comparison step repeat the full four-week period with an adjusted ruleset rather than a partial re-run.

title: Advisory Acceptance by Traffic Density
text:
Q: Does controller acceptance of Aeromere's top-ranked advisory vary with sector traffic density?
A: Yes. Of the 38,600 confirmed conflicts logged in 2025, controllers accepted Aeromere's top-ranked resolution advisory without modification in 61.4% of cases, modified the suggested heading or altitude before issuing it in 21.8% of cases, and disregarded the advisory entirely in favor of independent judgment in the remaining 6.8%. That fleet-wide acceptance figure drops noticeably in the busiest approach sectors. Controllers in high-density approach sectors report modifying advisories more often because a single suggested heading change can itself create a secondary conflict with a third aircraft the ranking did not weigh heavily enough. Aeromere's product team has an open proposal to factor tertiary-conflict risk directly into advisory ranking, though it remains unscheduled as of the current roadmap.

title: Deployment Footprint
text: Aeromere's twelve production sector deployments span four national air navigation service providers as of the most recent 2026 deployment registry, covering a combined 3.1 million square nautical miles of controlled en-route and approach airspace. The largest single deployment, an approach-control complex handling three converging major airports, processes a peak of 1,840 aircraft-hours of tracked traffic per day. Average sector commissioning time, from the start of shadow-mode operation to live cutover, is 9.4 weeks across the twelve deployments, ranging from 6 weeks at the smallest sector to 17 weeks at the largest. Hardware footprint per sector averages three redundant processing nodes, each provisioned with sufficient headroom to handle a full sector's peak traffic alone if the other two nodes fail simultaneously.

title: History: From Static Separation Rules to Intent-Blended Prediction
text: Aeromere began in 2016 as a much narrower tool: a static separation-rule checker that compared two aircraft's current positions against the applicable lateral and vertical minima and alerted controllers only once a violation had already occurred or was imminent within roughly ninety seconds. This reactive design offered controllers little advance warning, and the earliest field trials found controllers were often already aware of a developing conflict well before the tool alerted them, making the initial version more a compliance record than a genuinely useful advisory aid. The first major redesign, shipped in 2018, added trajectory prediction using pure kinematic extrapolation, projecting each aircraft's position forward from current heading and speed alone. This extended lead time meaningfully but produced a false-positive rate the earliest adopting sectors found disruptive, since a kinematic-only projection assumed every aircraft would fly straight and level indefinitely, flagging conflicts that a controller could see would never materialize once an aircraft's filed turn or descent was accounted for. Reducing that false-positive rate became the team's central preoccupation for the next several years. The 2020 release introduced flight-plan intent as a secondary input, blended cautiously alongside the kinematic projection rather than replacing it outright, since flight-plan data itself proved unreliable during the deployment's first months, some aircraft flew routes that diverged meaningfully from their filed plan without updating it. Confidence-weighting the intent input, trusting it more as an aircraft's actual track increasingly matched its filed route and less when the two diverged, resolved most of that reliability problem and became the design pattern later extended to sensor fusion itself. Radar-ADS-B fusion, added in 2022, applied the same confidence-weighting philosophy to reconciling two disagreeing position sources rather than trusting either exclusively. The square-root protected-volume growth model, adapted from published trajectory-uncertainty research, replaced an earlier fixed-volume-per-look-ahead-band approach in the 2023 release, smoothing out artificial discontinuities in advisory urgency that controllers had found confusing near band boundaries. Looking back, the engineering team describes the shift from reactive violation-checking to forward-looking, intent-aware prediction as the change that transformed Aeromere from a compliance tool into an operationally trusted advisory system, and notes that nearly every later refinement, confidence weighting, sensor fusion, and smooth protected-volume growth, was in service of making that forward-looking prediction reliable enough for controllers to actually act on before a conflict became urgent. Engineers who worked on the 2018 and 2020 releases still describe the shift to confidence-weighted intent blending as the hardest problem the team has solved, harder than the later sensor-fusion work, since getting the blending weights wrong in either direction reintroduced one of the two failure modes the redesign was meant to eliminate.

title: Incident Postmortem: The June 2023 Boundary Handoff Conflict
text: On June 19th, 2023, two aircraft approaching a shared boundary between adjacent sectors, each running Aeromere independently, came within 2.1 nautical miles of each other, below the 5-nautical-mile en-route minimum, in an incident later attributed to a gap in cross-sector conflict prediction rather than any single controller error. Each sector's Aeromere instance correctly tracked the aircraft under its own control and correctly predicted that aircraft's trajectory, but neither instance had visibility into the other sector's aircraft until the standard handoff exchange occurred, which at the time happened only after the aircraft had already crossed the boundary. Because the two aircraft's converging trajectories only became a predictable conflict when both were considered together, and that joint view did not exist until after the boundary crossing, Aeromere in both sectors flagged the developing conflict only 90 seconds before the predicted loss of separation, well under the 4.6-minute average lead time the system typically achieved for single-sector conflicts. The receiving sector's controller issued an emergency heading change with roughly 70 seconds of margin, resolving the conflict without an actual loss of separation, though well inside the safety margin the system was designed to provide. The investigation found that boundary-adjacent traffic represented a blind spot in Aeromere's architecture: trajectory prediction and conflict detection ran entirely within a single sector's instance, with no mechanism for two adjacent instances to exchange projected trajectories before an aircraft physically crossed the boundary. The remediation, which took approximately five months to design, test, and deploy, introduced a boundary-buffer zone extending 15 nautical miles into each sector, within which trajectory projections are shared continuously between adjacent Aeromere instances rather than only at formal handoff. A secondary finding noted that the 90-second lead time, while far below average, still exceeded the minimum lead time judged survivable by the safety board's existing risk model, meaning the system had technically stayed within its designed safety envelope even while performing far below its typical effectiveness. The safety board nonetheless classified the incident as a significant finding given how close the margin came in practice, and mandated the boundary-buffer remediation as a condition of continued certification for any multi-sector deployment. Adjacent-sector trajectory sharing has since been extended to all production deployments with a shared boundary, and no comparable boundary-blind-spot incident has been recorded since the fix was deployed. The board's review also required a dedicated boundary-conflict shadow-mode evaluation before certifying any future multi-sector deployment, folded into the commissioning checklist. Engineering leadership has since cited the incident as a clear example of a risk that passed every sector's safety review while still representing a real systemic gap, since no single instance had a defect on its own; the defect existed only between two correctly functioning instances.

title: Comparison Footnote: Approach-Sector Lateral Minimum
text: A footnote in Aeromere's original 2022 benchmark comparison against a competing conflict-detection tool noted, briefly, that approach control sectors typically tighten the lateral minimum to 2 nautical miles given denser, slower traffic and more precise radar coverage close to the airport, a detail used only to explain why the competing tool's results were not directly comparable across airspace classes. Expanded here for readers who saw only the abbreviated footnote: the competing tool did not vary protected-volume sizing by airspace class at all, applying the same en-route-calibrated volume uniformly. Its false-positive rate in approach sectors, exactly the regime Aeromere's class-aware ruleset targets, was substantially inflated relative to its own en-route performance.
